title: Time Value of Money
simple_html:
Money has a time value: a dollar in your hand today is worth more than a dollar promised next year. You could invest today's dollar and earn a return, and a future dollar carries the risk it never arrives. Time value of money (TVM) is the toolkit for comparing cash amounts that occur at different times.

The core idea

To compare cash across time you move it to a common date using an interest rate. Moving money forward in time is compounding (finding future value). Moving money backward is discounting (finding present value). The interest rate that does the moving is sometimes called the discount rate or required return.

Why it matters

Almost every financial decision — loans, savings, investments, valuing a company, choosing a project — requires adding up cash flows that happen at different times. You cannot simply sum them; you must first convert them to the same point in time.

Key takeaways

  • Future value: FV = PV x (1 + r)^n — grow today's money forward.
  • Present value: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n — bring future money back to today.
  • Compounding means you earn interest on interest, so growth accelerates over time.
  • An annuity is a series of equal payments (like a loan repayment or pension); special formulas value the whole stream at once.
  • Higher rates or longer time shrink present value and grow future value.

If you save 1,000 today at 8% for 10 years, it becomes 1,000 x 1.08^10 = about 2,159. Reverse the logic to find what a future amount is worth now.
intermediate_html:
The time value of money (TVM) rests on one principle: cash flows occurring at different dates are not directly comparable and must be translated to a common point in time using an interest (discount) rate that reflects opportunity cost and risk.

Single sums: compounding and discounting

Future value grows a present amount forward: FV = PV(1 + r)^n. Present value brings a future amount back: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n. The term 1/(1+r)^n is the discount factor. Note the inverse relationship: PV falls as either the rate r or the horizon n rises.

Multiple cash flows and annuities

The value of any stream is the sum of the values of its parts. An ordinary annuity pays a constant amount C at the end of each period:

PV = C x [1 - (1 + r)^-n] / r and FV = C x [(1 + r)^n - 1] / r.

An annuity due pays at the start of each period; multiply either formula by (1 + r). A perpetuity pays C forever: PV = C / r. A growing perpetuity (cash growing at g) is PV = C / (r - g) — the basis of the dividend growth model in valuation.

Nominal vs. effective rates

When compounding happens more than once a year, the stated (nominal) annual rate understates true growth. The effective annual rate is EAR = (1 + i/m)^m - 1, where i is the nominal annual rate and m the number of compounding periods per year. A 12% nominal rate compounded monthly gives EAR = (1 + 0.12/12)^12 - 1 = 12.68%.

Worked example: a car loan

You borrow 20,000 to be repaid in 48 equal monthly installments at a 9% nominal annual rate (0.75% per month). Solve the annuity PV formula for C:

20,000 = C x [1 - (1.0075)^-48] / 0.0075. The annuity factor is [1 - 0.6986]/0.0075 = 40.18, so C = 20,000 / 40.18 = 497.70 per month. Total paid = 48 x 497.70 = 23,890; the 3,890 excess is interest — the time value the lender charges.

How to apply it

  1. Draw a timeline. Place every cash flow at its period; sign inflows positive, outflows negative.
  2. Pick the right tool: single sum, ordinary annuity, annuity due, or perpetuity.
  3. Match the rate to the period: monthly cash flows need a monthly rate and monthly n.
  4. Discount to today (PV) to compare alternatives, or compound to a future date (FV) to find an accumulated balance.

TVM is the engine underneath nearly everything else in finance: bond and stock valuation, NPV in capital budgeting (Lesson 4), the cost of capital (Lesson 6), and loan amortization. Master the four building blocks — single sum, annuity, annuity due, perpetuity — and most problems become a matter of identifying which pattern applies.
full_html:
The time value of money (TVM) is the foundational concept of finance: because capital is productive and the future is uncertain, a unit of currency available today is worth more than the same unit received later. Every valuation in finance is ultimately an application of TVM — moving cash flows along a timeline with a rate that captures opportunity cost and risk.

Conceptual foundation

Two forces give money its time value. First, opportunity cost: cash held today can be invested to earn a return, so deferring receipt forfeits that growth. Second, risk and preference: future cash is uncertain, and individuals generally prefer present to future consumption (Fisher, 1930). The interest rate r is the price that reconciles these: it is simultaneously the reward for waiting and the rate at which the market discounts the future. In equilibrium it embeds the real risk-free rate, expected inflation, and a risk premium (the decomposition formalized in Lesson 5 via the CAPM).

The mechanics

Compounding moves cash forward: FV = PV(1 + r)^n. The exponential form reflects that interest earns interest — the defining feature distinguishing compound from simple interest, where FV = PV(1 + rn). Over long horizons the gap is dramatic. Discounting is the inverse: PV = FV/(1 + r)^n. The discount factor DF = (1 + r)^-n is monotonically decreasing in both r and n, so distant or heavily discounted cash flows contribute little to present value — a result with deep implications for how markets value long-dated and risky assets.

Valuing streams of cash flows

By the value-additivity principle, the present value of a stream is the sum of the present values of its components: PV = Sigma CF_t / (1 + r)^t. Several patterns admit closed-form solutions:

InstrumentPresent value
Ordinary annuity (n payments of C, end of period)C x [1 - (1+r)^-n]/r
Annuity due (payments at start)Ordinary annuity PV x (1 + r)
PerpetuityC / r
Growing perpetuity (growth g, g < r)C / (r - g)
Growing annuityC/(r-g) x [1 - ((1+g)/(1+r))^n]

The growing perpetuity is the analytical core of the Gordon (1962) dividend discount model and of the terminal-value calculation in discounted-cash-flow valuation; the annuity formulas underlie bond pricing, mortgage amortization, and lease analysis.

Compounding frequency and continuous compounding

When interest compounds m times per year, the effective annual rate is EAR = (1 + i/m)^m - 1. As m approaches infinity the limit yields continuous compounding: FV = PV x e^(rt) and PV = FV x e^(-rt). Continuous compounding is standard in derivatives pricing (e.g., Black-Scholes) because of its analytical tractability. The distinction between the annual percentage rate (APR), a simple nominal rate, and the EAR, the true periodic-compounded rate, is a frequent source of error and of consumer-protection regulation.

Worked example: bond and amortization combined

Consider a 1,000 face-value bond paying a 6% annual coupon (60 per year) for 5 years, when the market requires 8%. Its price is the PV of an annuity of coupons plus the PV of the redemption: P = 60 x [1 - 1.08^-5]/0.08 + 1,000 x 1.08^-5. The annuity factor is [1 - 0.6806]/0.08 = 3.9927, so coupons = 60 x 3.9927 = 239.56; redemption = 1,000 x 0.6806 = 680.58; price = 920.15. The bond trades at a discount because its coupon (6%) lies below the required return (8%) — a direct demonstration of the inverse price-yield relationship that follows from discounting.

For amortization, each loan payment splits into interest (rate x outstanding balance) and principal (the remainder); early payments are interest-heavy and later ones principal-heavy, because the balance — and thus the interest charge — declines over time.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Mismatched period and rate: the single most common error is applying an annual rate to monthly cash flows. Always convert both r and n to the same period.
  • Confusing ordinary annuity with annuity due: timing of the first payment changes the value by a factor of (1 + r).
  • Treating APR as EAR: a 12% APR compounded monthly is a 12.68% EAR; ignoring this understates true cost.
  • Using the growing-perpetuity formula when g >= r: the formula diverges and is economically meaningless; perpetual growth cannot equal or exceed the discount rate.
  • Adding nominal cash flows across periods: cash at different dates is incommensurable until discounted.
  • Ignoring that simple and compound interest diverge sharply over long horizons.

Cross-links

TVM is the indispensable prerequisite for the rest of the course. Lesson 4 (Capital Budgeting) applies discounting to derive NPV and IRR; Lesson 5 (Risk, Return & CAPM) supplies the risk-adjusted discount rate; Lesson 6 (Cost of Capital) computes the firm-wide rate r used in valuation; and equity and bond valuation rest entirely on the annuity and growing-perpetuity formulas developed here. As Brealey, Myers and Allen (2020) emphasize, mastering TVM mechanics is the gateway to all of corporate finance.
